title: St. Silas, Companion of the Apostle Paul & The Holy Martyrs of Alexandria
narrative:
On the thirtieth and final day of Hamle, the Ethiopian Church commemorates Saint Silas, one of the most significant and least celebrated figures in the apostolic mission of the New Testament. Silas was a leading member of the Jerusalem community sent with Paul and Barnabas to Antioch to deliver the decision of the Jerusalem Council, and he became Paul's primary companion on the second missionary journey — accompanying him through Macedonia and Achaia, sharing his imprisonment at Philippi where they sang hymns in the dungeon until an earthquake broke their chains, and continuing with him through the major Aegean mission that planted Christianity in Thessalonica, Beroea, and Corinth. He is the Silvanus of Paul's letters and the Silvanus identified in the First Letter of Peter as the scribe through whom that letter was written.

The Holy Martyrs of Alexandria bring this final day of Hamle into contact with the North African martyrological tradition — the great cloud of witnesses who died for the faith in Alexandria and its hinterland during the various persecutions of the Roman period. Alexandria was the intellectual capital of the ancient Christian world and also one of the sites of the most intense persecution: its community included the Catechetical School of Clement and Origen and also the blood of Potamiaena, of Dionysius, of Peter of Alexandria and countless others. The Ethiopian Church, formed in the Alexandrian theological tradition, commemorates the Alexandrian martyrs as ancestors in the faith — those who purchased with their blood the freedom within which the Ethiopian church received and transmitted the Gospel.

Silas and the Martyrs of Alexandria close the month of Hamle as they opened it — with a meditation on the communal, collaborative, anonymous character of the apostolic mission. Silas is barely visible in most tellings of the Pauline story, yet without him the second missionary journey would have been different in ways that cannot be calculated. The Alexandrian martyrs are mostly nameless, yet without their witness the theological tradition that shaped all subsequent Christianity would not have survived to be transmitted. The Ethiopian Synaxarium ends this month with the reminder that the work of the Gospel depends on the Silases and the unnamed Alexandrians as much as it depends on the Pauls and the Clements — that the visible and the invisible, the named and the unnamed, are equally essential to the Body of Christ. May the intercession of all the saints of Hamle accompany the faithful through this blessed month. Amen.
